coverage_notes: Covers the foundational LHTL research (Levine & Stray-Gundersen 1997, 2001), modern meta-analyses on altitude training efficacy, optimal hypoxic dose via network meta-analysis, erythropoietic mechanisms, elite runner case studies, and a 25-year retrospective on Olympic applications. Gap: no dedicated paper on altitude tent vs natural altitude comparison RCT or individual responder genetics.

papers.title: "Living high-training low": effect of moderate-altitude acclimatization with low-altitude training on performance
papers.notes: The seminal LHTL study. 39 runners randomized to high-low (live 2500m, train 1250m), high-high (live and train 2500m), or low-low control for 4 weeks. High-low group showed significant improvement in 5000m time trial, increased VO2max and red cell mass, while maintaining training velocity. Established the LHTL paradigm that dominates elite endurance training today.

papers.title: "Living high-training low" altitude training improves sea level performance in male and female elite runners
papers.notes: Follow-up to the 1997 landmark study, this time in elite runners (14 men, 8 women). 27 days of living at 2500m and training at 1250m improved 3000m time trial performance by 1.1% with one-third achieving personal bests. Confirmed LHTL works even in already highly-trained athletes, with a 3% VO2max increase and 1 g/dl hemoglobin increase.

papers.title: "Living High-Training Low" for Olympic Medal Performance: What Have We Learned 25 Years After Implementation?
papers.journal: International Journal of Sports Physiology and Performance
papers.notes: Comprehensive 25-year retrospective on LHTL implementation for Olympic performance. Covers 10 key lessons: optimal dose (>12h/day at 2000-2500m for 3-4 weeks), individual response variability, iron status requirements, training-load monitoring, wellness tracking, timing relative to competition, natural vs simulated hypoxia, adaptation robustness, broad athlete applicability, and method combinations.

papers.title: Effect of altitude training on the aerobic capacity of athletes: A systematic review and meta-analysis
papers.notes: Meta-analysis of 17 publications comparing altitude vs sea-level training. Altitude training produced significantly higher VO2max (SMD=0.67, p<0.001) and hemoglobin levels (SMD=0.50, p=0.013). Provides pooled effect sizes confirming altitude training's benefit for aerobic capacity across multiple endurance disciplines.

papers.title: Impact of Altitude Training on Athletes' Aerobic Capacity: A Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis
papers.notes: Most recent meta-analysis (2025) of 13 RCTs with 276 participants aged 18-35. Found altitude training improves aerobic capacity primarily through hematological adaptations and time trial performance, though impact on VO2max per se was minimal. LHTH approach and interventions >3 weeks were most effective. Searched 8 databases following PRISMA guidelines.

papers.title: Optimal type and dose of hypoxic training for improving maximal aerobic capacity in athletes: a systematic review and Bayesian model-based network meta-analysis
papers.notes: Large network meta-analysis of 59 studies (1821 athletes) using Bayesian modeling to compare all major hypoxic training paradigms. Ranked LHTL + low-altitude training as the most effective for VO2max improvement. Differentiates optimal protocols for athletes vs non-athletes (IHIT ranked best for athletes, IHT for non-athletes). Provides the strongest comparative evidence for choosing between altitude training methods.

papers.title: The Effects of Altitude Training on Erythropoietic Response and Hematological Variables in Adult Athletes: A Narrative Review
papers.notes: Comprehensive review of erythropoietic mechanisms underlying altitude training (covering 1997-2017 literature). EPO peaks within 1-3 days of altitude exposure then gradually returns to baseline. Key finding: even significant EPO increases do not guarantee improved hematological variables at sea level. Identifies critical moderators including hypoxic dose, iron stores, training load, genetics, and infection status.

papers.title: Improved race performance in elite middle-distance runners after cumulative altitude exposure
papers.journal: International Journal of Sports Physiology and Performance
papers.notes: Studied 7 elite middle-distance runners (VO2max 71.4 ml/min/kg) using a combined approach of simulated LHTL and natural altitude training camps over multiple years. Cumulative altitude exposure improved competitive race performance by 1.9% (90% CL: 1.3-2.5%). Demonstrates that repeated altitude blocks have additive benefits and supports periodized altitude training across a season.
